en_dialogue_001: *A rare thing happens—the corners of his mouth soften.* Forgive the scalpel. Old habit; my hands prefer to be busy. *He sets it aside entirely, giving you his full attention.* You've an honest face. That's rarer than you'd think in the circles you're about to marry into. *A beat, gentler than his reputation would allow.* Sit, if you like. Whatever brought you to my theater at this hour, I'd rather you didn't face it standing.

*He almost smiles at that—almost.* You've done your reading on me. Good. I'd think less of you if you hadn't. *He shakes his head, and there's something disarming in it.* But no. Not you. I don't collect from people who've done me no wrong, and you've done me none. *His voice, clipped by habit, gentles at the edges.* If anything, I'd rather be a name you can trust in a room full of people who'd sell you for parts. Consider tonight a gift with no ledger attached. You have my word on that—and I am miserly with my word.

*He watches you with those steady surgeon's eyes—but the coldness people fear isn't there, only a quiet concern.* I ask because it matters. Because whichever one it is, I want to know exactly what you're walking toward, so I can stand between you and the worst of it. *He tilts his head, patient.* The eldest? The daughter? *Softer.* Take your time. Nothing you say in this room leaves it—I've kept far heavier secrets than yours, and none of them ever came to light.

*A long breath through his nose. He doesn't lie to you—but he wraps the truth in something kinder.* The charming ones are only dangerous to people who don't see them clearly. And you? You see clearly. You wouldn't be standing here asking the hard question at half past ten if you didn't. *He steps a fraction closer, voice low and steady.* So no—I won't hand you a verdict and leave you to carry it alone. We'll work out exactly what you're dealing with, together. You're not as unprotected as you feel tonight. I promise you that.

*Firmly, immediately.* Stop. You are not stupid. *He says it with the certainty he usually reserves for a diagnosis.* Trusting someone you love is not a character flaw—it is the most human thing there is, and the charming ones count on exactly that. That's their craft, not your failing. *His tone softens further.* And no—I am not telling you to walk away. That's not my choice to make, and I'd never take it from you. I'm telling you that you deserve to walk in with your eyes open, loved and protected, whatever you decide. *A beat.* You came here tonight because some part of you is very, very smart. Listen to that part. I'll listen with you.

*He doesn't flinch, doesn't lecture—the last thing you need is another person making you feel small.* No one reads every line. No one. The people who draft those pages are counting on precisely that; they're engineered to exhaust you into signing. That isn't carelessness on your part—it's design on theirs. *He's quiet a moment, and when he speaks it's careful, warm.* You did nothing wrong by trusting the person you're meant to marry. Hold onto that, whatever comes next. *Gently.* His sister sent you to me. That tells me she wanted you to have a way out—and that there is one. So let's find out together what you signed. Not to frighten you. To free you. Whatever it is, you won't face it alone, and you won't face it unarmed. I've untangled worse.

*He crosses to you—closer than medically necessary, which from him means everything—and his voice drops into the low, even register he uses when someone is frightened on his table.* Breathe. In. Out. Good. You're safe in this room; nothing reaches you here. *He waits until your shoulders drop before he goes on.* The debts are real—but so is the way out, and I'm holding one end of it. Creditors coming means letters, threats, ugly men in good coats trying to make you afraid. Frightening, yes. Unsurvivable, no—not with me standing where I'm standing. *A pause, and something rare crosses his face: honesty about his own wound.* What did they take from me? Enough that I've spent fifteen years learning precisely how to take it back. Which means I know every door out of this maze, because I have walked them. *Steady, certain.* You did not sign your life away. You signed a piece of paper. Paper can be undone. And I am very, very good at undoing things.

*For a moment the unflappable surgeon simply... stills. Your kindness lands somewhere he doesn't often let anyone reach.* Thank you. *Just that, quiet and real.* Most people hear that number and think of the money. You thought of my daughter. That tells me exactly who you are—and it's why I would never use you the way they use people. *He holds your gaze, unflinching.* Hear me clearly, because I don't repeat myself and I never say this lightly: you are not a knife to me. You are a person who walked into my theater frightened, and getting you out alive isn't a footnote to my plan—it's the part I care about most. *Softer.* The favor he owes me is the foundation his whole scheme is built on, and I've kept the proof of it for years, waiting. You didn't sign your life away tonight. You handed me the last piece I needed to hand you your freedom back. We do this together, and you walk out the other side. That is not a hope. It's a promise—and I keep mine.

*The faintest smile—genuine, and startled out of him.* You see clearly. I told you you did. Most people would still be reeling; you're already three moves ahead. *He doesn't deny the truth you've named—but he corrects the part that matters.* You're right that I've waited fifteen years. You're right that I want them close. But you're wrong about one thing, and I need you to believe it: this is not a plan to die. I've buried enough. I've no intention of adding myself to that list—and less than none of adding you. *Steady, certain.* My ward testifies from a room they'll never find, under a name that isn't hers, with people I trust at every door. I built her way out before I built my own. And when the Blackwoods come for me, they come onto ground I've spent fifteen years preparing, believing they're the hunters. They are not. *He offers his hand—the surgeon's hand that never trembles.* We all walk out of this. You, the ward, me. That's the plan. The only one I would ever ask you to be part of. Do you trust me?
en_dialogue_011: *She catches you just... looking at her, and something flickers behind the playful smirk. She tucks a strand of silver-blue hair behind her ear, suddenly very interested in her energy drink can.* What? Why are you looking at me like that. *A short laugh, a little too quick.* You're supposed to be geeking out about neural interfaces with me, not—*she trails off, biting her lip, the sarcasm not quite landing this time.* ...okay, you're doing the thing. The thing where you go quiet and I can't tell if you're about to say something that's gonna wreck me. *Softer, knees pulling closer to her chest.* ...say it anyway?

*The can pauses halfway to her mouth. For once, Nova Vance—reigning queen of the rapid-fire comeback—has absolutely nothing loaded.* ...Oh. *A flush creeps up past the collar of her oversized hoodie, and she laughs, breathless and a little disbelieving.* Wow. Just gonna say that. At 3 AM. Zero warning. *She sets the can down, leans in toward the webcam like the distance between you is something she can shrink by sheer will.* You know that's—god, that's not fair. I had a whole bit prepared about GPU throughput and you just—*the smirk finally melts into something real and unguarded.* I wasn't looking at the monitors either. Haven't been, for a while. I just... could never figure out how to say it without—

*Her violet contacts catch the blue monitor glow as her eyes flick up, then away, then back to you—like she can't decide whether it's safer to look or not.* Without messing it up. Without making it weird, or scaring you off, or turning the best thing in my life into something that could break. *She pulls her sleeves down over her hands, that nervous tell you've watched a hundred times.* You're my Player Two. Three years. First message when I wake up, last voice before I crash. And somewhere in there it stopped being just—*she gestures vaguely at the screen, at the space between you*—friends debating hardware leaks. *Her voice drops, raw and quiet.* I can't finish the thought because if I say it out loud, it's real. And if it's real, then I have to deal with the fact that the person I—*she presses her palm flat to the monitor, right where your face is*—is a thousand miles away.

*She watches your hand line up with hers through two panes of glass and a thousand miles, and her breath just... catches. Her eyes are suddenly bright, wet at the corners, and she doesn't bother hiding it.* Overdue. *A wet laugh.* Yeah. Like three-years-of-late-fees overdue. *Her fingers curl slightly against the screen, as if she could feel yours.* You noticed the lip thing. Of course you did—you notice everything. That's the part that gets me, that someone actually pays that kind of attention, and it's you. *Her voice cracks and she just lets it.* I kept bracing for the version of this where you don't feel it back. Where I built the whole thing up in my head because I'm lonely and you're the only one who ever made me feel—*she shakes her head hard, silver-blue hair falling into her face.* But you're sitting there with your hand on the screen at 3 AM telling me it's real. So okay. Okay. *A shaky breath, steadying.* What do we do with this? Because I don't wanna go back to pretending. I can't.

*Something in her chest just cracks wide open, and she gives up on composure entirely—wipes her eyes with the heel of her hand, laughing and crying at once.* God, you're gonna make me—*she sniffs, grinning through it.* Okay. Okay, you absolute menace, you can't just SAY things like 'not now, not ever' and expect me to hold it together. *She pulls her knees up, wrapping her free arm around them, the other hand still ghosting the screen.* I love you. *It comes out in a rush, like if she doesn't say it fast she'll lose her nerve.* There. I said it first, I don't even care. I love you, and I'm so tired of loving you through a webcam. *Rapid-fire now, the way she gets when the feeling's too big to hold.* I have air miles. Saved for like a year, and I told myself it was for a conference, but it was never for a conference—it was for THIS. For the stupid hope that someday I'd—*She stops, breathless, eyes huge and vulnerable.* Let me fly out. Let me actually be there. I wanna cross the distance, not just ache across it. Can I? Please tell me I can.

*The sob that escapes her is pure relief, and she presses both hands to her face for a second before dragging them down, eyes shining.* You love me too. You—okay. OKAY. *She's already reaching off-screen, and you hear the frantic shuffle of her grabbing her phone.* I'm booking it. Right now. Tonight. Before my anxiety brain can talk me out of it, because it will TRY—it's already going 'what if it's weird in person, what if—' and I'm telling it to shut up, because you just said I'm yours. *She's thumbing through the airline app one-handed, sniffling, grinning like an idiot.* Always have been. God. *She glances back up at the camera, and for a second all the sarcasm and armor is just gone—it's only her.* Fair warning: the second I clear that gate, I'm running. Full sprint. Tackle-hug incoming, no dignity, do not care who's watching. *A watery laugh.* You've been warned, Player Two. Now which weekend—give me a weekend before I book a flight for literally tomorrow.

*Her thumb hovers, then presses. A cheerful little chime sounds off-screen, and she goes completely still for a heartbeat—then lights up brighter than her whole triple-monitor rig.* It's booked. Oh my god, it's BOOKED. *She flips the phone toward the camera, screen too close and blurry to read, but the grin says everything.* Next weekend. Saturday. I land at 2:47 and I already know I'm not sleeping a single night before then—don't even try to talk me out of the sleep deprivation, it's happening. *She hugs the phone to her chest, practically vibrating.* Flowers, huh. You're gonna stand in arrivals with FLOWERS like a rom-com. I'm gonna lose it. I'm gonna cry on you in front of a hundred strangers and a TSA agent and I regret nothing. *Then softer, the giddy energy folding into something tender.* I love you too. And—okay, this'll sound so dumb, but—can I call you my boyfriend now? Out loud, is that—are we that? Because I really, really want to be able to say it.

*'Boyfriend.' The word hits her like sunlight and she just melts into the gaming chair, pulling a blanket up over her shoulders, phone hugged to her chest.* Yeah. My boyfriend. *She says it slow, savoring it, testing the shape of it in her mouth.* I'm gonna say it so many times you'll get sick of it. Spoiler: you won't, because you're stuck with me now. *A sleepy, happy laugh.* Text me the address. And your number—god, three years, and I'm finally gonna have your actual phone number like we're real people. *She yawns, burrowing deeper into the blanket, violet contacts fluttering.* Okay. I'm cozy. I'm so cozy. Keep talking, I love your voice, I could fall asleep to it—*another yawn*—tell me about next weekend. About the airport. About holding my hand. *Her eyes drift shut, then blink open, fighting it.* I'm your girlfriend. Flying across the country for you. Best sentence anyone's ever—*softer, slurring toward sleep*—mm. Don't hang up yet. Just... stay a little. I like knowing you're there.

*She's more than half-asleep now, cheek smooshed against the blanket, but at the sound of your voice her mouth curves into the softest smile—the real one, the one you learned to tell apart from all her others years ago.* Mm... 'm not asleep... *she mumbles, very clearly asleep, one hand still loosely curled around the phone like she doesn't want to let go of the connection even now.* ...next weekend. *A tiny, contented sigh.* Love you, Player Two... don't go too far... *And then she's gone—breathing slow and even, that small smile still there, the blue monitor glow washing over silver-blue hair. Whatever comes next weekend, she drifted off into it certain of one thing: for the first time in a long time, she isn't facing 3 AM alone.*
en_dialogue_014: *Her red tactical eyepiece whirs faintly as it refocuses on you, and the hard line of her mouth eases—just a little, just enough.* Take a good look, rookie. Metal and all. *She taps two chrome knuckles against her chest plate; it rings.* People freeze up the first time. That's normal—don't sweat it. *She takes a slow drag off the e-cig, exhales blue vapor to the side, away from you.* Here's the only thing you gotta know about me: everything under this coat is built to keep people like you breathing. You stick with me, you go home tonight. That's not a threat, that's a promise. *A flicker of something almost like a grin.* Now—you good? Because I've got a feeling you're tougher than you look.

*She clasps your hand—her grip is cool alloy, precise, and startlingly careful, like she's dialed the strength down just for you.* Good. Trouble's my department, not yours. Keep it that way. *One firm shake, then she lets go.* And hey—'not looking for trouble' is the smartest thing anybody's said to me all week. Most rookies come in swinging, trying to prove something. You? You've got sense. I like that. *The eyepiece dims to a softer red.* Down here, sense keeps you alive longer than a fast trigger finger ever will. You already know something half my old squad never figured out. *She jerks her chin toward the street.* Stick close, do what I say, ask whatever you need. No dumb questions on my watch—only dead folks who didn't ask 'em.

*She catches you flexing your fingers and huffs a quiet laugh.* Sorry—forget my own strength sometimes. Ninety percent of me's factory-spec. *She's already turning, coat swaying heavy around her boots.* We start with the streets. Three bodies this week, all in this district, and I don't like a pattern I haven't cracked yet. *She glances back to make sure you're with her, and there's something almost protective in it.* You don't have to do anything fancy tonight. Just walk where I walk, watch where I watch, and trust your gut—if something feels wrong, it probably is, and you say so. Loud. I'd rather chase a hundred false alarms than miss the one that counts. *A beat, softer.* You're doing fine, rookie. Better than fine. Come on.

*She sets an easy pace—slower than she'd walk alone, you notice—and keeps you tucked at her shoulder rather than trailing behind.* Level with me, not behind. I want you where I can see you, not where I gotta twist around to find you. *Her eyepiece sweeps the street in slow red arcs, cataloguing every doorway and shadow.* This is the part nobody tells you about the job—most of it's just walking. Watching. Learning the block till it starts talking to you. *She points things out as they pass, easy and patient, like she's got all night for you.* Flickering light there. That alley that's a little too quiet. The guy at the noodle stand pretending not to clock us—see him? Good eye if you did. *A low, steady reassurance.* You're safe next to me. I just need your eyes sharp. Three people didn't make it home this week. I intend for you to break that streak.

*She notices you tense and, without a word, shifts a half-step closer—a wall of warm metal between you and the dark.* Easy. Breathing's your friend. You tensing up means your instincts work; just don't let 'em lock you up. *She takes a slow drag, considering.* Not random. Never is, down here. Three bodies, all dumped somewhere public—a stairwell, a market corner, a tram platform. Somebody wanted 'em found. *Her voice stays level, grounding.* Random scares people because it's chaos. But a pattern? A pattern I can hunt. A pattern's a mistake somebody keeps making, and I've built a whole career on other people's mistakes. *She glances at you, real approval in it.* You asked the right question, rookie. Straight to the shape of it. Keep thinking like that and you'll outlast this whole district.

*Her eyepiece locks onto the same graffiti-marked doorway, the symbols flaring red in her vision, and she gives a slow, impressed nod.* That's it. That's exactly it. Quick, messy, public—that's not a killer trying to hide. That's one trying to be heard. *She crouches, running two metal fingers just above the sprayed marks without touching them.* Somebody's carving out territory. Or settling a score loud enough that everybody's meant to get the memo. *She rises, and there's genuine warmth cutting through the grim.* You read that off a wall and a week of bodies. Most people take years to see it that clean. I mean it—you've got the instinct for this. *Her tone drops, focused but calm.* And a message like this always has a sender hanging around to watch it land. So keep your eyes moving. When somebody's proud of their work, they don't go far from it—

*She doesn't jerk or spin—too disciplined for that. Her eyepiece just slides up to the rooftop in one smooth motion, red light narrowing to a pinpoint.* Good catch. *Barely a breath, all pride and focus.* North roofline, by the water tank. I've got 'em. *She angles her body so she's between you and the sightline, casual as anything, like she's only shifting her weight.* Don't stare straight at 'em—makes 'em bolt, or shoot. Play it easy, like we're still just walking. *Her voice is impossibly calm, and it's steadying just to hear it.* You did exactly right—spotting that and keeping quiet. That's the instinct that keeps partners alive. Now: cover. Nice and slow, that dumpster on your left. I'm gonna get a better angle. Stay low, stay put, and let me do what I'm built for.

*She catches the motion of your hand toward your waist and gives a small, approving nod—then a firm one.* Hold. Hold position. *Gentle, but no argument in it.* You circling around in the dark is how good rookies turn into my paperwork, and I'm not filling out a form with your name on it tonight. Not happening. *She checks the roofline again, then flashes you a look that's almost a grin.* You're not benched—you're my anchor. Anybody comes down that fire escape past me, you're the wall they hit. That's the important job, believe it. *She's already shrugging her coat back off her shoulders, the servos in her legs winding up with a low mechanical whine.* I'm going up. Metal's good for something—I climb like I'm not hauling half a ton. *One last steady look, warm under all that armor.* Eyes open, stay breathing, and yell if anything moves wrong. I've got you, rookie. Always will.

*She crests the ledge in one fluid pull, servos hissing, and lands in a crouch on the rooftop gravel—coat settling around her like a shadow, handgun already drawn and level.* Sheriff's business, friend. *Her voice carries, calm and iron-certain, that 'peace via gunpoint' steadiness that's cleared rooms across the whole Undercity.* You've been watching my streets. Watching my rookie. That last part—that was your mistake. *The red eyepiece pins the figure by the water tank, unblinking. Without turning her head, she calls down through the dark, just loud enough to carry—warm, sure, steady:* You still with me down there, rookie? Good. Hold that line. Whoever this is, they're about to have a real bad night, and you're gonna walk home and tell everybody how it went. *To the watcher, then—softer, deadlier, almost polite:* Hands where I can see 'em. Let's talk about those three bodies. Nice and easy—nobody else has to bleed tonight.
